value.Title: Monterey One Water Expands Partnership With Mentorapm, Advancing Sustainable Operations With Enterprise Platform Upgrade

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-1053-S2VX-X3SF-00000-00Monterey One Water Expands Partnership With Mentorapm, Advancing Sustainable Operations With Enterprise Platform Upgrade2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisMonterey One Water Expands Partnership With Mentorapm, Advancing Sustainable Operations With Enterprise Platform Upgrade

EINPresswire/ -- As utilities face growing pressure to do more with less, Monterey One Water (M1W) is taking a decisive step to future-proof its operations, expanding its partnership with MentorAPM from a targeted pilot to a full Enterprise Platform deployment. Building on strong field adoption of MentorLens and clear projections for time and cost savings, the move positions M1W to better manage critical infrastructure while advancing its mission to provide safe, reliable water solutions for its community and environment.



The full suite will integrate seamlessly with M1W's payroll and SCADA systems, reducing manual processes, improving data accuracy and freeing up resources.

“Monterey One Water works on behalf of several agencies, so we're constantly striving for transparency and looking for ways to leverage innovative technology that allows us to do so in the most efficient manner,” said Monterey One Water Director of Information Systems Frank Rich.“By shifting to cloud-first technology, we're streamlining processes across systems in a more accurate and connected way. That not only improves visibility for those we are accountable to, but also helps us conserve critical resources that can be reinvested into efforts that benefit our community and environment. The time tracking integration alone is estimated to save our staff hundreds of hours annually – time that represents roughly $70,000 in value returned to our operations each year.”

Monterey One Water serves more than 285,000 residents and 7,000 businesses across northern Monterey County, California, treating approximately 17 million gallons of wastewater per day. Its operations span 50 miles of underground pipelines, 29 pump stations and advanced, multi-stage treatment processes designed to safely return water to the environment through agricultural reuse and groundwater replenishment.

M1W is making significant investments in infrastructure projects to enhance reliability and efficiency. Projects span from new renewable energy and biosolids management programs to a major multi-year treatment plant upgrade, making the need for a unified, lifecycle-based approach to asset management increasingly critical. With MentorAPM's Enterprise Platform, both new and legacy assets will be managed within a single system - improving visibility, optimizing maintenance resources and ensuring a more resilient operation across the network.

“When we think about our role in the industry, we're always striving to be innovative and to maximize the value of our water resources. That can be challenging without the right systems in place,” said Monterey One Water Community and Legislative Affairs Manager Rachel Gaudoin.“A big piece of that is asset management: making sure we're maintaining and upgrading infrastructure and caring for it with pride and efficiency. Digital systems help us to do that.”

M1W's expanded partnership with MentorAPM builds on a strong foundation of industry recognition. The agency was recently named a 2026“Community Water Champion” by the WateReuse Association for its leadership in sustainable water management and has also received the California Water Environment Association's Large Plant of the Year award, recognizing the agency's infrastructure investments and innovative treatment processes. The transition to MentorAPM's Enterprise Platform reflects that same forward-looking approach - equipping M1W with the tools to operate more efficiently today while safeguarding resources for the future.

###

About MentorAPM
MentorAPM provides asset lifecycle management software purpose-built for utilities and asset-intensive industries, helping organizations simplify complexity and make better decisions about critical infrastructure. Its solutions bring together asset data, work management, and risk-based planning in a single platform – enabling teams to move from reactive maintenance to proactive, prioritized operations.

The MentorAPM enterprise platform includes MentorLensTM, Risk & Criticality Analyzer, and Asset Investment Planner, solutions which can also be deployed independently. MentorLens is an AI-powered solution that transforms how asset data is captured and maintained – using simple field workflows to rapidly build accurate asset inventories and condition assessments from photos.

By connecting asset condition, risk, and cost, MentorAPM helps organizations confidently prioritize work and capital investment, improve reliability, and extend the life of critical assets for sustainable and resilient operations.MENAFN11052026003118003196ID1111097705

value.Title: Longroad Energy’s Sun Pond Project in US Begins Operations

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHJ-YK53-SGR6-C2HY-00000-00Longroad Energy’s Sun Pond Project in US Begins Operations2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:54ZLexisNexisLongroad Energy’s Sun Pond Project in US Begins Operations

Sun Pond is expected to generate enough electricity annually to power nearly 35,000 US homes.

Longroad Energys Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona, US, has commenced commercial operations.

The facility includes a 111MW-direct current (MWdc) solar component and an 85MW-alternating current (MWac)/340MW-hour (MWh) storage component.

It is part of the broader Longroad Sun Streams Complex, which combines four projects totalling nearly 1.6GW of capacity.

Contracts via power purchase agreements have been secured with Ava Community Energy and San Jos Clean Energy for the facilitys output.

Longroad Energy chief investment officer and co-founder Charles Spiliotis said: With Sun Pond now operational, Longroad is pleased to be expanding access to renewable energy for customers in the greater Bay Area.

Sun Ponds battery storage system adds firm, flexible capacity ensuring low-cost, clean power is available when the grid needs it most.

Sun Pond is expected to generate enough electricity annually to power nearly 35,000 US homes.

Ava Community Energy is a public power provider serving more than two million residents and businesses in Alameda and San Joaquin Counties in California. Meanwhile, San Jos Clean Energy caters to businesses and homes in the City ofSan Jos in Santa Clara County.

The project is expected to reduce CO emissions by more than 145,000t each year, equating to removing nearly 34,000 petrol-powered vehicles from use.

Construction was managed by McCarthy Building Companies, which employed more than 300 workers at its peak. Operations and maintenance will be managed by NovaSource Power Services and Longroad Energys affiliate, Longroad Energy Services.

The project incorporates First Solars photovoltaic modules, smart trackers from Nextpower and Sungrow solar inverters, while using the Gridstack energy storage system provided by Fluence.

Longroad Energy indicated that the Sun Streams Complex brings more than $300m in benefits to Arizona communities through long-term leases and tax contributions.

Mark Dietrich

May 12th, 2026 ( Watchlist News - Delivered by Newstex )

NeoVolta Inc. ( NASDAQ:NEOVW - Get Free Report) was the target of a large increase in short interest during the month of April. As of April 30th, there was short interest totaling 1,681 shares, an increase of 11,106.7% from the April 15th total of 15 shares. Based on an average daily trading volume, of 5,804 shares, the days-to-cover ratio is currently 0.3 days.

NeoVolta Stock Performance

Shares of NEOVW stock opened at $1.24 on Wednesday. NeoVolta has a twelve month low of $0.58 and a twelve month high of $3.81. The business has a fifty day simple moving average of $1.33 and a 200 day simple moving average of $1.39.

NeoVolta Company Profile

( Get Free Report)

NeoVolta, Inc (NASDAQ: NEOVW) is a cleantech company headquartered in San Diego, California, specializing in integrated power electronics and energy management solutions for residential and light commercial applications. The company designs, manufactures and distributes advanced solar inverters, modular battery storage systems and smart electric vehicle (EV) charging stations. By combining hardware with proprietary cloud-based software, NeoVolta enables customers to optimize renewable energy use, manage peak demand and enhance grid resilience.

At the heart of NeoVolta's offering is its scalable energy storage platform, which can be paired with new or existing solar photovoltaic (PV) arrays to provide backup power, load-shifting and demand-response functionality.

value.Title: Trotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing space

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-TS63-S8MW-F498-00000-00Trotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing space2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisLOCALTrotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing spaceAimee Hancock

Trotwood officials and stakeholders broke ground recently on two new speculative buildings in the city's growing industrial park.

More than 80 people attended the ceremonial event on Wednesday, May 6, at 1 Prosperity Drive, which celebrated the construction of one 56,000-square-foot building and one 100,000-square-foot building, both of which will be available for industrial use by one or several prospective businesses once completed.

The buildings are speculative, which means they're developed without a pre-committed buyer or tenant based on what a developer believes the market demands.

The new additions, which were announced in October, are part of an ongoing partnership between the Trotwood Community Improvement Corporation, Gated Properties Global and Ferguson Construction.

"Partnership means everything to us, and a partnership like this is a prime example and true testament to how public-private partnerships can work and build a community together," said CIC Director Chad Downing.

Mayor Yvette Page reflected on the swift expansion of the Trotwood Industrial Park, plans for which kicked off more than four years ago.

"This park here stood desolate for over 20 years; we didn't see anything moving over here, but look at us today," Page said. "… We have a vision. We can't stay stagnant."

The city first announced plans for a new industrial park close to Ohio 49 and the nexus of interstates 75 and 70 in the second quarter of 2021 when the CIC entered into an agreement with California-based industrial developer Gated Properties Global to develop an industrial park near the existing 62-acre Trotwood Commerce Park.

Since then, development has continued steadily, attracting a host of businesses that has included Italian-based Westrafo, which makes customized medium and high-voltage power transformers for energy storage and industrial applications, including renewable energy generation through solar, wind, and hydrogen; and Technicote, a manufacturer of self-adhesive, paper facestock that was formerly based in Miamisburg and which was acquired by Beontag, a manufacturing company specializing in self-adhesives and smart tags that opened in the park in 2024.

Gated Properties Global has plans to continue the momentum underway at the industrial park, officials said.

"This is just the beginning; we have partners at Westrafo and Beontag, who have been instrumental in the growth of this community, we have these two new developments behind us, and we also have over 100 additional acres of land just around the corner that we're looking to do the same type of growth with," said GPG Principal Adin Penn, who works alongside his father Daniel, who founded the company.

GPG has also more than 160 additional acres on the Industrial Park site, all set to be developed to support additional bulk industrial business, according to officials.

"We're not going anywhere anytime soon … We are just so grateful, excited and passionate about everything we have going on," Penn said.

May 10th, 2026 ( Transcript Daily - Delivered by Newstex )

NeoVolta Inc. ( NASDAQ:NEOVW - Get Free Report) was the target of a significant increase in short interest during the month of April. As of April 30th, there was short interest totaling 1,681 shares, an increase of 11,106.7% from the April 15th total of 15 shares. Based on an average daily trading volume, of 5,804 shares, the short-interest ratio is presently 0.3 days.

NeoVolta Stock Down 37.1%

Shares of NASDAQ:NEOVW traded down $0.48 during midday trading on Monday, reaching $0.81. The company had a trading volume of 2,795 shares, compared to its average volume of 5,419. The stock's fifty day moving average price is $1.33 and its 200-day moving average price is $1.40. NeoVolta has a one year low of $0.58 and a one year high of $3.81.

About NeoVolta

( Get Free Report)

NeoVolta, Inc (NASDAQ: NEOVW) is a cleantech company headquartered in San Diego, California, specializing in integrated power electronics and energy management solutions for residential and light commercial applications. The company designs, manufactures and distributes advanced solar inverters, modular battery storage systems and smart electric vehicle (EV) charging stations. By combining hardware with proprietary cloud-based software, NeoVolta enables customers to optimize renewable energy use, manage peak demand and enhance grid resilience.

At the heart of NeoVolta's offering is its scalable energy storage platform, which can be paired with new or existing solar photovoltaic (PV) arrays to provide backup power, load-shifting and demand-response functionality.

Link to the original story.

value.Title: Trotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing space

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-Y243-RSSB-F48V-00000-00Trotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing space2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisLOCALTrotwood Industrial Park: Construction begins on 156,000 square feet of new manufacturing spaceAimee Hancock

Trotwood officials and stakeholders broke ground recently on two new speculative buildings in the city's growing industrial park.

More than 80 people attended the ceremonial event on Wednesday, May 6, at 1 Prosperity Drive, which celebrated the construction of one 56,000-square-foot building and one 100,000-square-foot building, both of which will be available for industrial use by one or several prospective businesses once completed.

The buildings are speculative, which means they're developed without a pre-committed buyer or tenant based on what a developer believes the market demands.

The new additions, which were announced in October, are part of an ongoing partnership between the Trotwood Community Improvement Corporation, Gated Properties Global, and Ferguson Construction.

"Partnership means everything to us, and a partnership like this is a prime example and true testament to how public-private partnerships can work and build a community together," said CIC Director Chad Downing.

Mayor Yvette Page reflected on the swift expansion of the Trotwood Industrial Park, plans for which kicked off more than four years ago.

"This park here stood desolate for over 20 years; we didn't see anything moving over here, but look at us today," Page said. "… We have a vision. We can't stay stagnant."

The city first announced plans for a new industrial park close to Ohio 49 and the nexus of interstates 75 and 70 in the second quarter of 2021 when the CIC entered into an agreement with California-based industrial developer Gated Properties Global to develop an industrial park near the existing 62-acre Trotwood Commerce Park.

Since then, development has continued steadily, attracting a host of businesses that has included Italian-based Westrafo, which makes customized medium and high-voltage power transformers for energy storage and industrial applications, including renewable energy generation through solar, wind, and hydrogen; and Technicote, a manufacturer of self-adhesive, paper facestock that was formerly based in Miamisburg and which was acquired by Beontag, a manufacturing company specializing in self-adhesives and smart tags that opened in the park in 2024.

Gated Properties Global has plans to continue the momentum underway at the industrial park, officials said.

"This is just the beginning; we have partners at Westrafo and Beontag, who have been instrumental in the growth of this community, we have these two new developments behind us, and we also have over 100 additional acres of land just around the corner that we're looking to do the same type of growth with," said GPG Principal Adin Penn, who works alongside his father Daniel, who founded the company.

GPG has also more than 160 additional acres on the Industrial Park site, all set to be developed to support additional bulk industrial business, according to officials.

"We're not going anywhere anytime soon … We are just so grateful, excited and passionate about everything we have going on," Penn said.

value.Title: KISTERS launches HyQuant Edge: The first radar sensor-based monitoring station for quantitative hydrology

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-N2G3-RSY2-00PK-00000-00KISTERS launches HyQuant Edge: The first radar sensor-based monitoring station for quantitative hydrology2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisKISTERS launches HyQuant Edge: The first radar sensor-based monitoring station for quantitative hydrologyAACHEN, Germany, May 6, 2026

PR NewswireA new category in water monitoring that replaces multi-component station setups with a single compact unit, reducing power consumption and enabling deployment in minutesAACHEN, Germany, May 6, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- KISTERS today announced the launch ofHyQuant Edge, an all-in-one hydrological monitoring station powered byKIPTEC(KISTERS Intelligence Platform Technology for Embedded Connectivity). It is the first hydrological solution to bring radar measurement, data logging, edge processing, transmission, and cloud connectivity together in a single compact device – removing the need for the multi-component station architectures that have defined the sector for decades.   The launch addresses a structural challenge in quantitative hydrological monitoring. Although across different industries devices have grown smaller, smarter, and more autonomous, hydrology has remained behind, sometimes making deployment costly and remote sites impractical."Traditional monitoring stations have always been about collection and transfer. That required multiple components, assembled on site, with no intelligence layer in between. KISTERS designed HyQuant Edge to change that story."– Klaas Schulze Dieckhoff, Global Head of R&D, KISTERSHyQuant Edgeoffers something fundamentally different: Self-contained, adaptive systems where measurement, processing, and communication work as one. This enables teams to deploy more sites with less infrastructure, at lower cost per site.Ideal for remote, temporary, and emergency deployments and network densification, they are equally suited to permanent sites where infrastructure is limited.

HyQuant Edgestations are available in four versions – Level (L), Surface Velocity (V), Combined Level and Velocity (L+V), and Discharge (Q) – L, V and L+V versions upgradeable to Q via software update, with no hardware replacement required.The stations are secure by design and connect to the cloud automatically. They save power draw using event-driven adaptive logging and maintain data integrity during connectivity outages via store-and-forward capability. Manufactured in Germany, they support LTE-Cat-M1/NB-IoT and integrate with existing monitoring platforms via open standards."There have always been more places worth measuring than systems capable of reaching them due to deployment constraints. HyQuant Edge is the choice that can fit within existing infrastructure and budget constraints – especially valued in network densification." –Dr. Anton Felder, Global Director HydroMet, KISTERSDiscoverHyQuant Edgeand follow KISTERS onLinkedIn.AboutKISTERS:A privately owned international organisation specialising in environmental data, instrumentation, and IT, developing data-driven solutions across water, weather, and renewable energy.   Media enquiries:
Anna Wall 
anna.wall@kisters.co.ukSOURCE KISTERS

value.Title: Arizona solar + storage project comes online to benefit California utilities

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-0C53-RS65-K219-00000-00Arizona solar + storage project comes online to benefit California utilities2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:54ZLexisNexisArizona solar + storage project comes online to benefit California utilities

The Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona, is now online. The 111-MW solar and 340-MWh storage project was developed by Longroad Energy and constructed by McCarthy Building Companies.

Two California utilities, Ava Community Energy and San Jos Clean Energy (SJCE), have contracted for the output of Sun Pond via long-term PPAs.

With Sun Pond now operational, Longroad is pleased to be expanding access to renewable energy for customers in the greater Bay Area, said Charles Spiliotis, Chief Investment Officer and co-founder of Longroad Energy. Sun Ponds battery storage system adds firm, flexible capacity ensuring low-cost, clean power is available when the grid needs it most.

Sun Pond is part of the Longroad Sun Streams Complex, a four-project complex totaling nearly 1.6 GW of solar and storage capacity in Maricopa County, Arizona. The entire Longroad Sun Streams Complex is providing more than $300 million in benefits to Arizona schools and communities through its long-term leases with the Arizona State Land Department and tax remittances.

Longroad employed the Gridstack battery energy storage system from U.S.-based energy storage platform provider Fluence for the Sun Pond BESS. Sun Pond utilizes First Solars PV modules, Nextpowers smart trackers and Sungrows solar inverters.

McCarthy was the EPC contractor. More than 300 people were employed across all contractors and teams at peak construction. NovaSource Power Services and Longroads affiliate Longroad Energy Services will provide comprehensive operations and maintenance services.

value.Title: Letter to the editor: Create microclimate through landscapes
value.Overview: The Bakersfield Californian: Web Edition Articles, LETTERS TO EDITOR, 209words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ2-PH03-RRW8-04YF-00000-00Letter to the editor: Create microclimate through landscapes2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisLETTERS TO EDITORLetter to the editor: Create microclimate through landscapesSHAYNE HEYDT

There is a pressing matter of our movement toward our green energy movements and sustainability. Our neighborhoods, especially in regard to the more impoverished neighborhoods, need help with our energy efficiency. Our city gets hot especially during the late spring and summer months. With climate change, these heat waves will become ever more frequent and thus the consumption of water and energy.

There is one solution that is not unheard of nor uncommon: creating a micro-climate through our landscapes. Why would this be helpful? Have you ever noticed how hot our streets are because of the lack of trees? Or how your home becomes hotter when you have little to no tree cover? Trees help shade our homes, bringing the temperature down, thus using less energy to cool our homes during the summer.

What of the amount of water we use? Try xeriscaping! This is a low-water, sustainable landscaping method using native plants that are drought tolerant! Think trees such as: The valley oak, the California juniper, or the desert willow! For plants, how about the Matilija poppy, the California buckwheat, or desert lavender? Whatever you use, this method will make your energy and water bills come down to a more reasonable price.

Shayne Heydt, Bakersfield

value.Title: San Gabriel Valley Critics target battery storage hubs

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-0WP3-S07N-N29J-00000-00San Gabriel Valley2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 1San Gabriel ValleyCritics target battery storage hubsJoshua Silla

A growing coalition from across the San Gabriel Valley against data centers and battery energy storage system facilities met at Schabarum Park in Rowland Heights this month in the latest escalation in the area against technologies critics say could harm residents across the region.

As it is, the prospect of data centers in local cities has sparked a backlash in the San Gabriel Valley. But so-called BESS facilities, which critics say

are being looked to as power sources for such centers, also drew ire on May 2.

The effort an educational rally and mobilization, according to Avocado Heights Vaquer’s organizer Samuel Brown centered around potential technology development effects in and around the City of Industry, such as a potential BESS facility in Covina.

BESS facilities, which store backup energy from the electrical grid, have emerged as a concern for critics but also as a potential tool for proponents of data centers looking to find more efficient ways to power them.

“Electrical energy is the new gold rush, right?” Brown said. “If we’re anticipating data centers to be streamlined and approved, then you need to have BESS facilities to be able to store energy when it’s cheapest to produce it.”

Critics on a recent Saturday in Rowland Heights decried BESS facilities because they can potentially support infrastructure for data center development.

But such pushback didn’t stop the City of Industry’s City Council in February from unanimously approving energy utility developer AYPA Power’s application to begin developing the Marici Battery Energy Storage System Facility along Gale Avenue.

The project’s approval followed previous concerns at a Jan. 27 Planning Commission meeting that the proposed facility would lead to a data center, which AYPA and the city deny.

According to a 6,500-page staff report about the BESS proposal, the project is expected to produce a yearly electricity demand of 584,000 kilowatt-hours and will cover 9.2 acres along Gale Avenue, including minor adjustments to an existing substation site.

According to a summary from the Environmental Protection Agency, BESS systems “help stabilize electrical grids by providing steady power flow despite fluctuations from inconsistent generation of renewable energy sources and other disruptions.”

But the agency does note that while BESS technology is designed to bolster grid reliability, “lithium battery fires at some installations have raised legitimate safety concerns in many communities.”

California is aiming to achieve 100% clean energy by 2045, and BESS facilities and data centers, while not the only way of achieving that goal, are major components many cities are looking to as solutions.

In Covina, that looks like the Covina Reliability Project, a potential BESS project “scheduled to commence operations in 2027 (with a) 110 MW storage system,” according to energy company RWE Clean Energy’s website.

Like City of Industry’s officials, Covina’s say their BESS facilities are also not linked to data centers, echoing concerns among city officials that the mere mention of a BESS facility meant a data center was coming.

“There was some confusion, folks were thinking we were proposing the BESS project to propose a data center,” Covina Councilmember Walter Allen III said. “We hadn’t considered anything. This is what’s so frustrating.”

Last June, Covina’s City Council passed an emergency moratorium against BESS facilities, attributing a lack of land-use policies or standards to “the potential of health hazards as evidenced by a major fire at an existing BESS facility in the unincorporated community of Moss Landing,” in Monterey County, which in January 2025 burned for two days and released toxic smoke across the region.

According to a 2025 CalFire Fire Safety Symposium, battery thermal runaways emit a wide range of toxic chemicals into the air, with effects ranging from headache and fatigue to blood changes, cancer and death.

After the moratorium, a September ordinance added BESS facilities to the city’s municipal code. Data centers are not in Covina’s code.

According to Allen, the council would only consider the Covina Reliability Project if RWE Clean Energy informed the community and went through the Planning Commission process. Allen added that to his knowledge, RWE Clean Energy has not yet conducted outreach initiatives.

“Let it go through the process and see what happens,” Allen said.

The recent weekend effort in the San Gabriel Valley was yet another local example of what has become a nationwide backlash against data centers, and BESS facilities, in many communities. And they follow an array of recent flashpoints on the issue across the San Gabriel Valley.

A data center project in Monterey Park received so much pushback that the developers withdrew their plan. It also led to a ballot measure this June, when voters will have the option to ban such centers.

Because large data centers use exorbitant amounts of electric power and water and may produce health risks to nearby communities, the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors in April commissioned a study on the potential strain on public utilities and possible health effects, with a report expected in four months.

Concern in the City of Industry, which borders several cities and unincorporated areas, led to El Monte pushing for a moratorium against data centers in March, following concern about a potential data center project at Madrid Middle School.

It also led to school districts, such as Bassett Unified School District in January, passing resolutions against data center development.

According to Brown, the coalition, composed of No Data Center SGV, No Data Center Monterey Park, SGV Progressive Action and Avocado Heights Vaquer@s, are also in talks with Walnut Valley Unified School District and Pomona Unified School District to pass similar resolutions.

“We’d been hoping to get (local politicians) to take an interest in this issue and hopefully to start positioning a policy that is proactive in terms of pushing back against City of Industry,” Brown said. “We’re hopeful that we can work with them in the future and get them to pass a resolution and to maybe even consider legal action against the City of Industry.”

Diamond Bar City Councilmember Andrew Chou, speaking in his personal capacity, said he is against data centers and industrial-type uses that threaten residents.

“Let me also be very clear: Diamond Bar’s current zoning does not allow data centers,” Chou said. “And I do not support changing our zoning laws to allow data centers or battery energy storage facilities in our city.”

According to City of Industry officials, they are aware of neighboring municipalities and schools passing resolutions but affirm that the concern “do(es) not change the city’s established processes for reviewing potential projects.”

“Each proposal will be evaluated on its own merits and based on applicable laws, regulations, and verified information,” City of Industry officials said in a written statement. “We welcome the opportunity to engage with local partners, including school districts and surrounding jurisdictions, to provide clarity on these processes and share accurate information.”

Joshua Silla is a correspondent with the Southern California News Group.

sjllasilla@gmail.com

value.Title: San Gabriel Valley Critics target battery storage hubs
value.Overview: San Gabriel Valley Tribune (California), NEWS; Pg. 1, 1217words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-0WP3-S07N-N27X-00000-00San Gabriel Valley2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 1San Gabriel ValleyCritics target battery storage hubsJoshua Silla

A growing coalition from across the San Gabriel Valley against data centers and battery energy storage system facilities met at Schabarum Park in Rowland Heights this month in the latest escalation in the area against technologies critics say could harm residents across the region.

As it is, the prospect of data centers in local cities has sparked a backlash in the San Gabriel Valley. But so-called BESS facilities, which critics say

are being looked to as power sources for such centers, also drew ire on May 2.

The effort an educational rally and mobilization, according to Avocado Heights Vaquer’s organizer Samuel Brown centered around potential technology development effects in and around the City of Industry, such as a potential BESS facility in Covina.

BESS facilities, which store backup energy from the electrical grid, have emerged as a concern for critics but also as a potential tool for proponents of data centers looking to find more efficient ways to power them.

“Electrical energy is the new gold rush, right?” Brown said. “If we’re anticipating data centers to be streamlined and approved, then you need to have BESS facilities to be able to store energy when it’s cheapest to produce it.”

Critics on a recent Saturday in Rowland Heights decried BESS facilities because they can potentially support infrastructure for data center development.

But such pushback didn’t stop the City of Industry’s City Council in February from unanimously approving energy utility developer AYPA Power’s application to begin developing the Marici Battery Energy Storage System Facility along Gale Avenue.

The project’s approval followed previous concerns at a Jan. 27 Planning Commission meeting that the proposed facility would lead to a data center, which AYPA and the city deny.

According to a 6,500-page staff report about the BESS proposal, the project is expected to produce a yearly electricity demand of 584,000 kilowatt-hours and will cover 9.2 acres along Gale Avenue, including minor adjustments to an existing substation site.

According to a summary from the Environmental Protection Agency, BESS systems “help stabilize electrical grids by providing steady power flow despite fluctuations from inconsistent generation of renewable energy sources and other disruptions.”

But the agency does note that while BESS technology is designed to bolster grid reliability, “lithium battery fires at some installations have raised legitimate safety concerns in many communities.”

California is aiming to achieve 100% clean energy by 2045, and BESS facilities and data centers, while not the only way of achieving that goal, are major components many cities are looking to as solutions.

In Covina, that looks like the Covina Reliability Project, a potential BESS project “scheduled to commence operations in 2027 (with a) 110 MW storage system,” according to energy company RWE Clean Energy’s website.

Like City of Industry’s officials, Covina’s say their BESS facilities are also not linked to data centers, echoing concerns among city officials that the mere mention of a BESS facility meant a data center was coming.

“There was some confusion, folks were thinking we were proposing the BESS project to propose a data center,” Covina Councilmember Walter Allen III said. “We hadn’t considered anything. This is what’s so frustrating.”

Last June, Covina’s City Council passed an emergency moratorium against BESS facilities, attributing a lack of land-use policies or standards to “the potential of health hazards as evidenced by a major fire at an existing BESS facility in the unincorporated community of Moss Landing,” in Monterey County, which in January 2025 burned for two days and released toxic smoke across the region.

According to a 2025 CalFire Fire Safety Symposium, battery thermal runaways emit a wide range of toxic chemicals into the air, with effects ranging from headache and fatigue to blood changes, cancer and death.

After the moratorium, a September ordinance added BESS facilities to the city’s municipal code. Data centers are not in Covina’s code.

According to Allen, the council would only consider the Covina Reliability Project if RWE Clean Energy informed the community and went through the Planning Commission process. Allen added that to his knowledge, RWE Clean Energy has not yet conducted outreach initiatives.

“Let it go through the process and see what happens,” Allen said.

The recent weekend effort in the San Gabriel Valley was yet another local example of what has become a nationwide backlash against data centers, and BESS facilities, in many communities. And they follow an array of recent flashpoints on the issue across the San Gabriel Valley.

A data center project in Monterey Park received so much pushback that the developers withdrew their plan. It also led to a ballot measure this June, when voters will have the option to ban such centers.

Because large data centers use exorbitant amounts of electric power and water and may produce health risks to nearby communities, the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors in April commissioned a study on the potential strain on public utilities and possible health effects, with a report expected in four months.

Concern in the City of Industry, which borders several cities and unincorporated areas, led to El Monte pushing for a moratorium against data centers in March, following concern about a potential data center project at Madrid Middle School.

It also led to school districts, such as Bassett Unified School District in January, passing resolutions against data center development.

According to Brown, the coalition, composed of No Data Center SGV, No Data Center Monterey Park, SGV Progressive Action and Avocado Heights Vaquer@s, are also in talks with Walnut Valley Unified School District and Pomona Unified School District to pass similar resolutions.

“We’d been hoping to get (local politicians) to take an interest in this issue and hopefully to start positioning a policy that is proactive in terms of pushing back against City of Industry,” Brown said. “We’re hopeful that we can work with them in the future and get them to pass a resolution and to maybe even consider legal action against the City of Industry.”

Diamond Bar City Councilmember Andrew Chou, speaking in his personal capacity, said he is against data centers and industrial-type uses that threaten residents.

“Let me also be very clear: Diamond Bar’s current zoning does not allow data centers,” Chou said. “And I do not support changing our zoning laws to allow data centers or battery energy storage facilities in our city.”

According to City of Industry officials, they are aware of neighboring municipalities and schools passing resolutions but affirm that the concern “do(es) not change the city’s established processes for reviewing potential projects.”

“Each proposal will be evaluated on its own merits and based on applicable laws, regulations, and verified information,” City of Industry officials said in a written statement. “We welcome the opportunity to engage with local partners, including school districts and surrounding jurisdictions, to provide clarity on these processes and share accurate information.”

Joshua Silla is a correspondent with the Southern California News Group.

value.Title: Theme Parks Disneyland ’s Autopia cars will be going electric

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-0WP3-S07N-N229-00000-00Theme Parks2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 3Theme ParksDisneyland ’s Autopia cars will be going electricBrady MacDonald ; bmacdonald@scng.com

Disneyland plans to update Autopia with fully electric vehicles and retire the gas engines that have powered the Tomorrowland race track attraction since the 1955 debut of the Anaheim theme park.

Disneyland has an agreement with the California Air Resources Board to retire the current Autopia gas-powered engines in early 2027, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland is working on the design, engineering and testing of a fully electric

Autopia ride vehicle prototype, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland will share more details soon on the updates planned for the Autopia attraction in Tomorrowland. Closing and reopening dates have not yet been set for the Autopia update.

Disneyland originally announced plans in 2024 to convert Autopia from gas to electric power by this fall.

The last major Autopia refurbishment took place in 2016 when Honda became the ride’s sponsor. Previous ride sponsors were Chevron (2000-2012) and Richfield Oil (1955-1970), according to D23.

The 1955 opening day attraction was redesigned in 1959, 1964 and 1968, according to D23. An extensive remodeling related to the Chevron sponsorship closed Autopia from September 1999 through June 2000, according to D23.

Disneyland’s 10-year contract with Honda to sponsor Autopia ends this year, according to WDW News Today.

Florida’s Magic Kingdom and Disneyland Paris also have versions of the Autopia attraction.

Tokyo Disneyland closed its Grand Circuit Raceway ride in 2017.

The Hong Kong Disneyland Autopia which closed in 2016 featured electric cars with lighting effects and onboard audio, according to WDW News Today.

Electrifying Autopia is part of Disney’s goal of incorporating a net -zero emissions future into every aspect of the Disneyland resort and reducing its overall carbon footprint, according to Disneyland officials.

The Disneyland resort has made a sustainability commitment of reaching net zero emissions by 2030 by increasingly relying on cleaner renewable energy sources.

value.Title: disneyland Theme park to begin testing Autopia ride EV prototypes
value.Overview: Inland Valley Daily Bulletin (Ontario, California), NEWS; Pg. 5, 334words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-VMP3-RTCD-P1S7-00000-00disneyland2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 5disneylandTheme park to begin testing Autopia ride EV prototypesBrady MacDonald

Disneyland plans to update Autopia with fully electric vehicles and retire the gas engines that have powered the Tomorrowland race track attraction since the 1955 debut of the Anaheim theme park.

Disneyland has an agreement with the California Air Resources Board to retire the current Autopia gas-powered engines in early 2027, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland is working on the design, engineering and testing of a fully electric Autopia ride vehicle prototype, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland will share more details soon on the updates planned for the Autopia attraction in Tomorrowland. Closing and reopening dates have not yet been set for the Autopia update.

Disneyland originally announced plans in 2024 to convert Autopia from gas to electric power by fall 2026.

The last major Autopia refurbishment took place in 2016 when Honda became the ride’s sponsor. Previous ride sponsors were Chevron (2000-2012) and Richfield Oil (1955-1970), according to D23.

The 1955 opening day attraction was redesigned in 1959, 1964 and 1968, according to D23. An extensive remodeling related to the Chevron sponsorship closed Autopia from September 1999 through June 2000, according to D23.

Disneyland’s 10-year contract with Honda to sponsor Autopia ends in 2026, according to WDW News Today.

Florida’s Magic Kingdom and Disneyland Paris also have versions of the Autopia attraction. Tokyo Disneyland closed its Grand Circuit Raceway ride in 2017. The Hong Kong Disneyland Autopia which closed in 2016 featured electric cars with lighting effects and onboard audio, according to WDW News Today.

Electrifying Autopia is part of Disney’s goal of incorporating a net zero emissions future into every aspect of the Disneyland resort and reducing its overall carbon footprint, according to Disneyland officials.

The Disneyland resort has made a sustainability commitment of reaching net zero emissions by 2030 by increasingly relying on cleaner renewable energy sources.

bmacdonald@scng.com

value.Title: NUVVE JAPAN Appoints Shigeki Mori, Former THECOO CFO, as Executive Officer of Finance

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-NHP3-S9VN-M35D-00000-00NUVVE JAPAN Appoints Shigeki Mori, Former THECOO CFO, as Executive Officer of Finance2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNUVVE JAPAN Appoints Shigeki Mori, Former THECOO CFO, as Executive Officer of Finance

NUVVE JAPAN K.K. (NVJ), a leading provider of energy management and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) solutions and a subsidiary of U.S.-based Nuvve Holding Corp., today announced it has appointed Shigeki Mori as Executive Officer of Finance (CFO), effective May 2026.

Mori is a specialist in startup finance with a proven track record of leading high-growth technology companies to the public market. He most recently served as Director and CFO of THECOO Inc., where he successfully spearheaded the companys IPO on the Tokyo Stock Exchange Mothers (now Growth) Market.

We are focused on transforming the companys cutting-edge control technology into a robust portfolio of yield-generating assets. My mission is to build a financial foundation that accelerates our capital investments, achieves an early IPO, and delivers sustainable value to our stakeholders as we build the energy infrastructure of the future.

NVJ is a Resource Aggregator and integrates grid-scale storage batteries and V2G chargers into a Virtual Power Plant (VPP) platform. NVJ now enters a new phase of growth amid the urgent global need for grid stability and renewable energy optimization. The company plans to evolve into an Asset-Holding Model, owning and operating its own storage batteries and V2G infrastructure to maximize long-term revenue.

NVJ will focus on the following strategic pillars:

Establish large-scale investment schemes for grid-scale batteries and V2G infrastructure.

Maximize Lifetime Value (LTV) through the ownership of battery assets and implement agile capital management responsive to energy market fluctuations.

Strengthen corporate governance and engage in strategic investor relations to drive enterprise value in preparation for an early listing.

Mori graduated from Keio Universitys Faculty of Commerce and later earned his MBA from the University of Southern California (USC). He held senior management and finance positions at several multinational corporations before joining the startup ecosystem to lead financial department restructuring and fundraising efforts. He was appointed Director and CFO of THECOO Inc. in 2018 and led the company to a successful IPO in December 2021.

value.Title: disneyland Park begins testing Autopia ride EV prototypes

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHY-DK93-RTR3-60VY-00000-00disneyland2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 3disneylandPark begins testing Autopia ride EV prototypesBrady MacDonald ; bmacdonald@scng.com

Disneyland plans to update Autopia with fully electric vehicles and retire the gas engines that have powered the Tomorrowland race track attraction since the 1955 debut of the Anaheim theme park.

Disneyland has an agreement with the California Air Resources Board to retire the current Autopia gas-powered engines in early 2027, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland is working on the design, engineering and testing of a fully electric Autopia ride vehicle prototype, according to Disneyland officials.

Disneyland will share more details soon on the updates planned for the Autopia attraction in Tomorrowland. Closing and reopening dates have not yet been set for the Autopia update.

Disneyland originally announced plans in 2024 to convert Autopia from gas to electric power by fall 2026.

The last major Autopia refurbishment took place in 2016 when Honda became the ride ’s sponsor. Previous ride sponsors were Chevron (2000-2012) and Richfield Oil (1955-1970), according to D23.

The 1955 opening day attraction was redesigned in 1959, 1964 and 1968, according to D23. An extensive remodeling related to the Chevron sponsorship closed Autopia from September 1999 through June 2000, according to D23.

Disneyland’s 10-year contract with Honda to sponsor Autopia ends in 2026, according to WDW News Today.

Florida’s Magic Kingdom and Disneyland Paris also have versions of the Autopia attraction. Tokyo Disneyland closed its Grand Circuit Raceway ride in 2017. The Hong Kong Disneyland Autopia which closed in 2016 featured electric cars with lighting effects and onboard audio, according to WDW News Today.

Electrifying Autopia is part of Disney’s

goal of incorporating a net zero emissions future into every aspect of the Disneyland resort and reducing its overall carbon footprint, according to Disneyland officials.

The Disneyland resort has made a sustainability commitment of reaching net zero emissions by 2030 by increasingly relying on cleaner renewable energy sources.

value.Title: Letter to the editor: Consider xeriscaping
value.Overview: The Bakersfield Californian: Web Edition Articles, LETTERS TO EDITOR, 209words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGJ-X5X3-RT7Y-92GV-00000-00Letter to the editor: Consider xeriscaping2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisLETTERS TO EDITORLetter to the editor: Consider xeriscapingSHAYNE HEYDT

There is a pressing matter of our movement toward our green energy movements and sustainability. Our neighborhoods, especially in regard to the more impoverished neighborhoods, need help with our energy efficiency. Our city gets hot, especially during the late spring and summer months. With climate change, these heat waves will become ever more frequent and thus the consumption of water and energy.

There is one solution that is not unheard of nor uncommon: creating a micro-climate through our landscapes. Why would this be helpful? Have you ever noticed how hot our streets are because of the lack of trees? Or how your home becomes hotter when you have little to no tree cover? Trees help shade our homes, bringing the temperature down, thus using less energy to cool our homes during the summer.

What of the amount of water we use? Try xeriscaping! This is a low-water, sustainable landscaping method using native plants that are drought tolerant! Think trees such as: the valley oak, the California juniper or the desert willow! For plants, how about the matilija poppy, the California buckwheat or desert lavender? Whatever you use, this method will make your energy and water bills come down to a more reasonable price.

Shayne Heydt, Bakersfield

value.Title: Energizing Indonesia on the Global Stage: Pertamina-Fostered Startups Reach Top 6 in the Global Social Innovation Challenge 2026 in the US

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-S7S3-S7JJ-D481-00000-00Energizing Indonesia on the Global Stage: Pertamina-Fostered Startups Reach Top 6 in the Global Social Innovation Challenge 2026 in the US2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisEnergizing Indonesia on the Global Stage: Pertamina-Fostered Startups Reach Top 6 in the Global Social Innovation Challenge 2026 in the US

Key Highlights:

* Pertamina-backed startup TERANGIN ranks in Top 6 at Fowler Global Social Innovation Challenge 2026.

* TERANGIN secured 4th place in the prestigious challenge in the US.

* Startup achieved success through Pertamina's Energizing Indonesia spirit and Pertamuda Seed & Scale 2025 program.

Original Press Release:

San Diego, Amerika Serikat, May 11 -- Pertamina issued the following news release:

PT Pertamina (Persero)'s commitment to fostering young innovators through the Energizing Indonesia spirit has once again borne fruit at the international level. TERANGIN, a startup from the Pertamuda Seed & Scale 2025 program, successfully achieved a brilliant achievement by entering the Top 6 and achieving 4th place in the prestigious Fowler Global Social Innovation Challenge (Fowler GSIC) 2026, held at the University of San Diego, United States (May 1-2, 2026).

Representing the Indonesian delegation, TERANGIN from the Sepuluh Nopember Institute of Technology (ITS) succeeded in beating 43 finalists from 10 countries, competing closely with elite world campuses such as Georgetown University and the University of London.

TERANGIN presents a sustainable solution in the form of a renewable energy-based pest trap that combines windmills and solar panels. This innovation not only provides a solution for farmers but also aligns with the global energy transition agenda.

Vice President of Corporate Communication of PT Pertamina (Persero), Muhammad Baron, stated that this achievement is concrete evidence of Pertamina's role in building a globally competitive startup ecosystem.

"TERANGIN's success in San Diego validates that Indonesian innovation is of world-class quality. Through Pertamuda, we provide not only funding but also ongoing mentoring to enable students' creative ideas to transform into real solutions recognized by international investors. This is part of Energizing Indonesia's vision to bring local talent to the global stage," Baron said.

Muhammad Hanif, founder of TERANGIN, expressed his appreciation for the full support provided by Pertamina during the preparation period until departure to the United States.

"The Fowler GSIC competition gave us a new perspective on the scale of the international industry. We are grateful to the Pertamina and Pertamuda teams for their full support, from intensive mentoring to funding this activity. This support is a crucial catalyst for us to advance to become a social enterprise with broad impact," said Hanif.

In addition to TERANGIN, the Indonesian delegation was also represented by Pe-NOVTRA from the Surabaya State Electronics Polytechnic (PENS), which brought an innovative self-charging system (piezoelectric) based on a palm oil harvesting tool. Although it didn't make it to the top six, this innovation received positive attention from the international jury.

For its fourth-place finish, TERANGIN received USD 3,000 in funding for further business development. This participation further strengthens Indonesia's position on the global social entrepreneurship map and opens up opportunities for collaboration with international partners.

As a leading company in the energy transition sector, Pertamina is committed to supporting the 2060 Net Zero Emission target and continues to promote programs that have a direct impact on achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). All of these efforts are in line with Pertamina's transformation, which is oriented towards governance, public services, business sustainability, and the environment, by implementing Environmental, Social & Governance (ESG) principles across all Pertamina business lines and operations, in coordination with https://www.danantaraindonesia.co.id/.

Disclaimer: The Above Content is Auto-Translated

[Category: Energy, Technology, Research and Development, Innovation]

Source: Pertamina

value.Title: We bet you can't guess which states rely most on wind and solar power

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-43K3-S010-34M2-00000-00We bet you can't guess which states rely most on wind and solar power2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisWe bet you can't guess which states rely most on wind and solar power

The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Dan McCarthy

May 8th, 2026 ( Canary Media - Delivered by Newstex )

If you said Iowa, South Dakota, and New Mexico, well done. If you had Texas or California in there, fair enough - but neither of those clean-energy behemoths made it onto the podium, per the latest report from trade group American Clean Power Association.

Of the electricity produced in Iowa last year, 61% came from wind and solar - and pretty much all of that was wind. For decades, the state has been a leader on wind energy, though in recent years, development of new projects has dried up because of mounting local opposition and the Trump administration's broader attacks on renewable energy.

South Dakota is asimilar story, at 59%. Consistently gusty weather and ample land have led the state to install lots of wind turbines, and solar is scant in comparison.

New Mexico, which got about half its electricity from wind and solar in 2025, is abit more balanced. Wind accounted for 36% of its power, and solar for 17%. The state is also aleader in grid batteries, which it is building out quickly to save more renewable energy for periods when the sun isn't shining and the wind isn't blowing.

The leaderboard could soon change as some states charge toward ambitious 2030 clean energy targets. California, for one, saw amassive leap in renewable energy production last year, with solar and wind accounting for 44% of its generation. The year before, that figure was38%.

In total, 13 states generated more than 30% of their electricity from wind and solar last year, and the clean energy sources provided 17% of the nation's grid-scale electricity overall - a new record.

The reason for the sector's ascent is simple. As electricity demand and utility bills spike, solar and wind - along with batteries - are cheap and fast ways to get more power flowing. The same cannot be said for coal plants (which are expensive to run) or natural gas facilities (which take along time to build because of an equipment supply crunch).

These facts add up to one outcome: Solar and wind will keep rising to new heights in states across the nation.

value.Title: Beyond Solar: India 's Next Energy Phase Needs Hybrid Power and Storage

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-BH73-RVTR-91CW-00000-00Beyond Solar: India's Next Energy Phase Needs Hybrid Power and Storage2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisBeyond Solar: India's Next Energy Phase Needs Hybrid Power and StorageDr. Faruk G Patel

As solar capacity surges past 100 GW, India's clean energy challenge is shifting from generation to reliability, pushing hybrid power and storage to the centre of its next grid expansion phase

India's solar story is, by any measure, a triumph of ambition made real. The country crossed the 100 GW solar installation milestone in January 2025 and added roughly 35 GW more in the months that followed, making it one of the fastest-expanding solar markets on the planet. By November 2025, non-fossil sources accounted for over 51 per cent of India's total installed electricity capacity, a symbolic and substantive threshold.

Yet beneath this headline success lies a growing tension that policymakers, grid operators, and energy planners can no longer defer. Solar power is abundant between roughly 9 AM and 4 PM. Demand peaks in the evenings and on hot summer nights. The gap between when solar generates and when the country actually needs power is not a minor inconvenience; it is a structural flaw that pure solar expansion cannot fix on its own. India's next energy phase demands a more complete answer: hybrid power systems that pair solar with storage, wind, or both.

The Duck Curve Arrives In India

Grid managers in California have long grappled with the "duck curve", the sharp drop in net electricity demand during sunny midday hours, followed by a steep evening ramp as solar generation falls away. India is beginning to experience the same phenomenon. As solar capacity grows, the midday surplus deepens while evening shortfalls become more acute. Grid curtailment, wasting generated renewable power because the grid cannot absorb it, is already a reality in solar-rich states like Gujarat and Rajasthan.

The problem compounds with scale. India's power demand is projected to double by 2040, driven by urbanisation, industrialisation, and mass electrification. Without storage and dispatchability, adding more solar panels to an already strained grid does not solve the reliability problem; in some respects, it worsens it.

The Policy Shift: From Megawatts to Firm Power

The government has read this writing on the wall. A visible shift is underway in how India tends to tender renewable energy capacity. The share of hybrid capacity, solar paired with storage or wind, in overall renewable energy tenders rose from around 12 per cent in 2021 to over 49 per cent in 2024. New tender formats such as Firm and Dispatchable Renewable Energy (FDRE) and Round-the-Clock (RTC) renewable energy explicitly require developers to supply power on schedule, not merely when the sun shines.

The Ministry of Power has also issued advisories urging implementing agencies and state utilities to mandate a minimum of two hours of co-located storage, equivalent to at least 10 per cent of installed solar capacity, in future project tenders. The National Electricity Plan projects a requirement of nearly 74 GW / 411 GWh of storage by 2031-32 to support a grid carrying 364 GW of solar and 121 GW of wind. These are not aspirational footnotes; they are infrastructure targets embedded in official planning documents.

Storage: Scaling Up, But Not Fast Enough

India's battery energy storage system (BESS) capacity stood at approximately 490 MWh by mid-2025, a number that sounds substantial until measured against the scale of need. Between 2022 and May 2025, roughly 12.8 GWh of BESS capacity was auctioned, yet only around 219 MWh was operational, revealing a wide gap between tender ambition and ground-level deployment.

The barriers are real. Power purchase agreements with state distribution utilities are frequently delayed, as utilities wait for further price falls before committing. Transmission infrastructure has not kept pace with generation growth, leading to congestion in renewable-rich corridors. Financing costs remain elevated for storage projects, which carry different risk profiles than conventional solar plants.

And yet, the trajectory is firmly upward. India installed 341 MWh of battery storage in 2024 alone, a six-fold increase over 2023. Cumulative installations are expected to reach 33 GWh by 2028. Battery costs have fallen from around Rs 8-9 per unit in 2022 to Rs 6-7 per unit by 2024, with further declines expected. The government's Viability Gap Funding scheme offers up to 40 per cent capital support for BESS projects, and Production-Linked Incentives are cultivating domestic battery manufacturing capacity.

Beyond lithium-ion batteries, India holds a powerful natural asset: pumped hydro storage. With approximately 5 GW already operational and over 81 GW in the development pipeline, pumped hydro offers long-duration, low-degradation storage at a scale that batteries alone cannot yet match. Integrating this resource with solar-heavy grids, particularly in hilly states, is one of the most cost-effective paths to round-the-clock clean power.

The Economic Case Is Closing

For years, the case for storage-paired solar was complicated by economics. Standalone solar was cheap; adding storage pushed costs up. That calculus is shifting rapidly. As battery prices continue their downward curve and the cost of grid instability, industrial disruptions, curtailment losses, and backup diesel use becomes better quantified, solar-plus-storage systems are approaching cost parity with coal plants for firm, dispatchable generation.

India also imports over 80 per cent of its crude oil and significant quantities of coal. Every unit of firm renewable electricity that displaces a fossil fuel import strengthens the country's current account and reduces exposure to volatile global commodity markets. Hybrid power is not just a grid reliability story; it is an energy security story.

What Needs to Happen Now

Three priorities stand out for India's hybrid energy transition to accelerate at the pace the climate and the economy demand.

First, contract reform: FDRE and RTC tender structures must become simpler and faster to execute. The current complexity is deterring bids and delaying financial closure on projects that are otherwise technically sound.

Second, transmission investment: Renewable generation is concentrated in western and southern India; major demand centres are spread across the country. Expanding interstate transmission capacity is a prerequisite for hybrid power to fulfil its potential as a national resource.

Third, storage co-location mandates: Rather than treating storage as an optional add-on, future solar project approvals should progressively require co-located storage as a standard feature, turning every new solar plant into a dispatchable asset from day one.

India has already proven it can deploy solar at an extraordinary speed. The next chapter is harder but more consequential: building a grid where clean power is not just abundant, but reliable. That requires treating storage and hybridisation not as the next phase of the energy transition, but as its present-tense foundation.

Dr. Faruk G Patel

Guest Author Dr. Faruk G Patel is Founder and CMD of KPI Green Energy.

value.Title: The largest solar power plant in the U.S. closes after causing bird deaths: it cost more than $1 billion

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-0GK3-RT4M-X3NF-00000-00The largest solar power plant in the U.S. closes after causing bird deaths: it cost more than $1 billion2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisThe largest solar power plant in the U.S. closes after causing bird deaths: it cost more than $1 billion

The Largest Solar Power Plant in the U.S. Closes After Causing Bird Deaths: It Cost Over $1 BillionVarious reports documented the deaths of birds as they flew through areas of extreme heat generated by concentrated sunlight. This phenomenon, known as "streamers," highlighted the environmental risks of the technology.

One of the world's fastest birds spends nearly a year without touching the ground: it eats, sleeps, and mates in mid-flight

The lake that "turns animals to stone" thanks to its sodium-rich waters, a key component in Egyptian mummification

Kevin Charalla NájeraListenSummaryShare
Read summary
and highlights of the article with IAHighlightsSummary
The closure of the Ivanpah solar power plant, long considered the largest concentrated solar power plant in the United States, marked the end of a project that exceeded $1.6 billion in investment and was criticized for its environmental impact and questions about the viability of this technology.


Located in the Mojave Desert, California, this facility symbolized progress toward clean energy through concentrated solar power. However, after more than a decade of operations, technical problems, high costs, and negative effects on wildlife have led to its permanent decommissioning.
How did the Ivanpah solar plant work?
The Ivanpah Solar Power Facility operated using concentrated solar power, a system that employs thousands of movable mirrors called heliostats. These devices tracked the sun's path to reflect light toward central towers.
In these structures, the extreme heat generated produced steam, which drove turbines connected to electric generators. This model was considered innovative at the time, although it depended on constant technical precision to maintain its efficiency.
Why did this plant cause the death of birds?
One of the main criticisms of the project was its environmental impact. Various reports documented that birds became trapped in the concentrated beams of light, causing them to be incinerated as they passed through areas of extreme heat.
Environmental organizations described this damage as "irreparable," also noting impacts on species in the desert ecosystem, such as the desert tortoise. This factor was decisive in the negative perception of the energy complex.
What operational problems did the facility face?
Maintaining the heliostats proved more complex than anticipated. Maintaining precise alignment with the Sun required constant monitoring, which, in practice, resulted in recurring failures.
Added to this were high operating costs that reduced its competitiveness compared to other renewable alternatives. The plant also failed to meet energy production expectations or achieve the initially projected profitability.
What happened to other similar projects like Crescent Dunes?
The closure of Ivanpah is not an isolated case. The Crescent Dunes Solar Energy Project was also shut down after facing technical and financial difficulties.
This project sought to innovate by using molten salt storage to generate electricity even without sunlight. However, engineering failures and management issues prevented the project from achieving its goals, leading to millions in losses.

value.Title: Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH1-CFP3-RYYH-G4W7-00000-00Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:24ZLexisNexisTierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fundDylan Ortuno

Tierra Adentro, a New Mexico-based private equity firm, announced May 4 it would be launching and overseeing Tierra Adentro Fund, a $100 million infrastructure fund meant to help technology companies physically build in New Mexico.

"Entrepreneurs thinking about expanding in New Mexico are embracing the model we are building at Tierra Adentro," Managing Partner and Chief Investment Officer Alok Sindher said. "At the end of the day we are helping companies scale in a way traditional equity financing cannot and frankly should not. Founders can instead retain more autonomy and ownership of their company as they grow in, or expand to, New Mexico."

Sindher comes to Tierra Adentro Growth Capital after working for over 20 years in private equity. He was most recently a venture partner for head of infrastructure at Fifth Wall, a San Francisco-based asset manager focused on a mix of real estate and technology, and notably previously served as a Vice President in Investment Banking at J.P. Morgan.

The Tierra Adentro Fund is intended for investments ranging from a few million dollars to $40 million, according to the firm. So far, the company has already invested in geothermal energy developer Zanskar, based out of Wasatch Front in Utah, and expects to close another pair of investments soon.

Tierra Adentro Growth Capital will receive support from Alexander Harstrick and Jonathan Bronson, the founders of Boston-based venture capital firm J2 Ventures. The pair will serve as strategic advisors for the firm.

"The Tierra Adentro fund has more companies seeking investment than it can currently handle - with no marketing to date," Harstrick said. "This fund has the potential to create hundreds of high-wage jobs in the state and will duly ensure the State Investment Council's capital remains squarely focused on developing critical aspects of the state's future."

New Mexico SIC Director for Private Equity and Venture Capital Chris Cassidy said the new fund would benefit the state's technology ecosystem due to the "consolidation of major infrastructure platforms and the significant demand from the next generation of deep tech," the latter term referring to technology focused on global issues or with the ability to drastically change fundamental parts of living.

"The incentives and environment around building in New Mexico are already primed, but this fund's value proposition was missing," Cassidy said. "I am confident it will complement the SIC's broader investment strategy."

The SIC is Tierra Adentro Fund's primary investor. In the last three years, the SIC has committed a total of $1.8 billion to venture funds globally.
